# Polytopes {#sec-polytopes}

::: {.content-hidden}
{{< include ../_macros.qmd >}}
:::

We briefly recall some standard definitions and results concerning polytopes in Euclidean space. For additional context, see @DH1998.

An $n$-dimensional polytope may be defined either as the convex hull of a finite set of points in $\Rb^n$ or, equivalently, as a bounded intersection of finitely many half-spaces. The $k$-dimensional faces of a polytope (for all $0 \leq k \leq n$) form a graded structure that will be useful when passing to more combinatorial descriptions.

Of particular importance are cubes and their higher-dimensional analogues. The $n$-cube, or *hypercube*, can be described as the Cartesian product of $n$ intervals, and its face lattice admits a simple description in terms of binary strings. This combinatorial regularity makes hypercubes a convenient testing ground for general constructions, even in settings where the underlying metric structure is modified or only partially specified.

We will frequently move between geometric and combinatorial viewpoints. In the former, notions such as length, angle, and orthogonality play a central role; in the latter, adjacency, incidence, and orientation data become primary. Later, these perspectives will no longer be entirely equivalent, and care will be required when translating between them. Firstly, we recall a fundamental result:

::: {#thm-pythagoras}
#### Pythagorean Theorem
For a right triangle with legs $a, b$ and hypotenuse $c$, we have 
$$
    a^2 + b^2 = c^2.
$$
:::
::: {.proof}
See, for example, @JJ2024.
:::

We use this Theorem and its generalizations from planar geometry implicitly when working in locally Euclidean coordinates, particularly when describing orthogonal decompositions and distance computations in $\Rb^n$. The role in more general settings will be qualified throughout these notes.
